title: Compliance & Safety
description: Stay inspection-ready every day — Virginia licensing requirements, the licensing binder, health and safety protocols, incident reporting, and emergency procedures for every scenario.

sections.title: Virginia Licensing & the Binder
learningObjectives: Know the Virginia Child Day Center license type, renewal cycle, and inspection pattern
Know all seven sections of the licensing binder and what belongs in each
Understand what 'tour-ready' means for your binder at all times
sections.content:
## Licensing Is Not a Once-a-Year Event

A Virginia licensing inspector can walk through your door unannounced at any time. Your binder, your staff records, your drill logs, your medication forms — all of it must be current and organized right now, not when you get a heads-up. Inspection-ready is a daily standard.

---

### Virginia Licensing Basics

| Item | Detail |
|------|--------|
| License type | Child Day Center (CDC) |
| Renewal cycle | Annual — know your site's expiration date |
| Inspections | Unannounced; typically 1–2 per year |
| Licensing specialist | Listed in your site's Vendor Directory |

> **A repeat violation is a leadership failure.** A first finding is correctable. The same finding on a second inspection means you didn't fix the root cause — and licensing treats it very differently.

---

### The Licensing Binder: Seven Sections

This binder must be complete, current, and accessible at all times — including during tours.

#### Section 1: License & Approvals
- Current Child Day Center license (posted visibly)
- Fire marshal approval letter
- Health department approval

#### Section 2: Staff Records
- Background check documentation for every staff member
- TB test results (2-step for new hires)
- Physical exam records (within required timeframe)
- Transcripts (if staff claim CDA or degree for licensing credit)
- Training certificates (child abuse, SIDS, medication administration, first aid/CPR)

#### Section 3: Child Records
- Enrollment forms for every enrolled child
- Current immunization records (Virginia schedule)
- Emergency contact forms
- Medical action plans (for children with allergies, asthma, or chronic conditions)

#### Section 4: Safety
- Fire drill logs (monthly — date, time, duration, issues noted)
- Tornado drill logs (monthly, alternating with fire)
- Lockdown drill logs (quarterly)
- Site evacuation plan with posted maps
- Emergency procedures card

#### Section 5: Health
- Medication Authorization Forms (MAF) for every child currently receiving medication
- Injury and accident logs
- Illness and exclusion policies

#### Section 6: Food Service
- Monthly menus
- Food handling certifications for applicable staff
- Kitchen inspection reports

#### Section 7: Policies
- Current parent handbook (signed acknowledgment on file for each family)
- Staff handbook (signed acknowledgment on file for each staff member)
- Operational policies document

---

### Keeping the Binder Current

The binder goes stale when:
- A new staff member starts and their records aren't filed within the first week
- A drill happens but isn't logged the same day
- A child's immunization record expires and no one follows up
- A medication authorization form expires and the child is still receiving doses

Your Day 16 monthly task is a full binder review — every section, every document, current or flagged for follow-up.

---

### What Licensing Inspectors Look For

Inspectors evaluate three things:

1. **Documentation** — is everything present, signed, and current?
2. **Ratios** — are classrooms within Virginia-required staff-to-child ratios at all times?
3. **Environment** — are spaces safe, clean, and appropriate for the age group?

The most common findings:
- Expired staff TB tests or physical exams
- Missing or incomplete drill logs
- Cleaning supplies accessible to children
- Expired medication authorization forms
- Ratios broken during transitions (drop-off, pick-up, outdoor play)

All of these are preventable with a consistent daily and monthly routine.

questions.question: How often are Virginia Child Day Center licensing inspections typically conducted?
options: Once a year, scheduled in advance
Unannounced; typically 1–2 times per year
Quarterly, with 48-hour notice
Only when a complaint is filed
questions.correctAnswer: Unannounced; typically 1–2 times per year
questions.explanation: Virginia licensing inspections are unannounced — inspectors can walk in at any time. This is exactly why 'inspection-ready' is a daily standard, not something you prepare for when you get a call. Typically 1–2 inspections per year, but the timing is unpredictable.

questions.question: Which binder section contains fire drill logs, tornado drill logs, and the site evacuation plan?
options: Section 2: Staff Records
Section 3: Child Records
Section 4: Safety
Section 5: Health
questions.correctAnswer: Section 4: Safety
questions.explanation: Section 4 (Safety) contains all drill documentation: fire drill logs, tornado drill logs, lockdown drill logs, the evacuation plan, and the emergency procedures card. Section 5 (Health) covers medication forms and injury logs. Mixing these up during an inspection creates problems.

questions.question: True or False: A staff member who claims a CDA credential only needs to provide verbal confirmation — transcripts are optional.
questions.correctAnswer: False
questions.explanation: False. If a staff member claims a CDA or college degree for Virginia licensing credit, transcripts must be physically on file in the licensing binder (Section 2: Staff Records). Verbal claims are not sufficient — the inspector will ask to see the document.

questions.question: What is the most important distinction between a first licensing finding and a repeat violation?
options: First findings are reported to admin; repeat violations are not
First findings require a 30-day correction plan; repeat violations require immediate closure
A repeat violation means the root cause wasn't fixed — licensing treats it much more seriously
Both are treated the same; only the number of findings in a year matters
questions.correctAnswer: A repeat violation means the root cause wasn't fixed — licensing treats it much more seriously
questions.explanation: A repeat violation is a leadership failure — it signals that a problem was identified, documented, and then allowed to happen again. Licensing treats repeat findings with escalating seriousness: higher fines, more frequent inspections, and potential license action. Fix the root cause the first time, not just the symptom.

sections.title: Daily Health & Safety Protocols
learningObjectives: Complete the morning and afternoon health and safety checklists from memory
Know the illness symptoms that require a child to go home and the return requirements
Understand the medication administration requirements and what triggers a compliance issue
sections.content:
## Safety Is a Habit, Not a Response

The checklists below aren't bureaucracy — they're the daily practice that keeps children safe, keeps your license clean, and catches problems before they become incidents. A director who skips the morning check is a director who finds out about a missing first aid kit only when someone needs it.

---

### Morning Safety Checklist (Before Children Arrive)

- **Bathrooms:** Soap, paper towels, toilet paper, functioning toilets and sinks in every bathroom
- **Playgrounds:** No hazards (broken equipment, debris, standing water), all equipment secure, gates locked
- **First aid kits:** Stocked and present in every classroom — check at least weekly that contents are complete
- **Bleach solution:** Made fresh daily, correct concentration (test with litmus paper), distributed to each classroom and labeled with today's date
- **Emergency exits:** All exits clear and unlocked from the inside — never prop, block, or lock an exit

---

### Afternoon Safety Checklist

- **Building perimeter walk:** Check for hazards, unlocked gates, trash or debris
- **Cleaning caddies:** Locked in every classroom — no exceptions
- **Cleaning supplies:** Not accessible to children anywhere in the building
- **Medications:** All stored securely in locked cabinet; log is current

---

### Illness Policy: When to Send a Child Home

Children must be sent home or not admitted if they have any of the following:

- Fever of **100.4°F or higher**
- Vomiting — 2 or more times in 24 hours
- Diarrhea — 2 or more watery stools
- Unexplained rash
- Pink eye or other contagious infection confirmed or suspected
- Lice — child may return after treatment and nit-free verification

#### Return-to-School Requirements

- **Fever:** Fever-free for **24 hours without medication**
- **Vomiting or diarrhea:** Symptom-free for 24 hours
- **Diagnosed contagious illness:** Doctor's note required before return

> When in doubt, call the parent. It is always safer and more defensible to send a child home than to keep a potentially ill child in a classroom with 19 others.

---

### Medication Administration

#### Requirements Before Any Medication Is Given

- **Prescription medications:** Current, signed Medication Authorization Form (MAF) on file; original bottle with child's name, dosage, and prescribing doctor's name
- **Over-the-counter medications:** Same requirements as prescription, plus a doctor's note
- **No expired medications** — verify the expiration date; return expired medications to parents
- **Storage:** Locked cabinet at all times; refrigerate if required
- **Every dose logged** on the MAF: date, time, dose given, staff initials

#### Staff Authorization

- Only staff who have completed **annual medication administration training** may give medications
- Director is responsible for verifying this training is current for every staff member
- If a trained staff member is unavailable, the director administers the medication — never an untrained person

#### Common Mistakes That Create Compliance Issues

- Child receives medication without a current signed MAF — this is a licensing violation
- MAF has expired (they're typically written for a school year) — obtain a new one before the next dose
- Medication given by an untrained staff member
- Dose not logged immediately after administration
- Medication stored outside the locked cabinet (even briefly)

---

### Bleach Solution Standards

Bleach solution is your primary classroom sanitizer and must be:

- Made **fresh every morning** — bleach loses effectiveness within 24 hours
- Tested with **litmus paper** to confirm correct concentration before distribution
- Labeled with the **date it was made**
- Distributed to every classroom in its labeled container
- Stored away from children — locked caddy when not in active use

questions.question: A child arrives at 8:00 AM and a parent mentions she vomited twice last night but 'seems fine now.' What do you do?
options: Admit her and monitor closely — she seems recovered
Admit her but notify the teacher to watch for symptoms
Do not admit her — she must be symptom-free for 24 hours
Call the pediatrician before making a decision
questions.correctAnswer: Do not admit her — she must be symptom-free for 24 hours
questions.explanation: Two or more vomiting episodes in 24 hours is an exclusion criterion — and the return requirement is 24 hours symptom-free. 'Last night' plus 'morning arrival' means the 24-hour window has not passed. Admit her and you've violated illness policy and exposed 19 other children. The parent may push back; hold the line kindly but firmly.

questions.question: Who may administer medications to children at Bright Beginnings?
options: Any staff member with parental permission
Only the director
Only staff who have completed annual medication administration training
Any licensed teacher with more than one year of experience
questions.correctAnswer: Only staff who have completed annual medication administration training
questions.explanation: Only staff with current annual medication administration training may administer medications — no exceptions. If no trained staff member is available, the director gives the medication. An untrained person administering medication is a licensing violation regardless of parental consent.

questions.question: True or False: Bleach solution made on Monday morning can be used through Friday as long as it is stored correctly.
questions.correctAnswer: False
questions.explanation: False. Bleach solution must be made fresh every single morning — it loses its sanitizing effectiveness within 24 hours. Each day's batch must be tested with litmus paper for correct concentration, labeled with that day's date, and distributed to classrooms. Previous days' solution should be discarded.

questions.question: A parent asks you to give their child a daily vitamin — no prescription, just a children's multivitamin. What is required?
options: Verbal permission from the parent is sufficient
A signed permission note from the parent on file
A signed Medication Authorization Form and a doctor's note
Nothing — vitamins are not considered medications under licensing
questions.correctAnswer: A signed Medication Authorization Form and a doctor's note
questions.explanation: Over-the-counter medications — including vitamins — require the same documentation as prescriptions: a current signed MAF and a doctor's note. Virginia licensing does not distinguish between a vitamin and a prescription based on the nature of the substance — if you're administering it to a child at school, you need the paperwork.

sections.title: Injury, Accident & Incident Reporting
learningObjectives: Know the correct response sequence for minor and major injuries
Understand when licensing must be notified and within what timeframe
Know what goes in every incident documentation entry
sections.content:
## Document Everything. Every Time.

Incidents happen in childcare. What separates well-run centers from poorly-run ones isn't the absence of incidents — it's the consistency and completeness of how they're handled and documented. Your documentation is your protection.

---

### Minor Injuries (No Medical Attention Required)

*Scratches, small bumps, minor falls, insect bites — injuries that are treated on-site.*

**Response sequence:**

1. Clean and treat the injury (bandage, ice, comfort)
2. Complete the **injury/accident log** immediately — paper form, on the spot
3. Call the parent to inform them — *before* pick-up, not at pick-up
4. Send a Tadpoles note with a photo if the injury is visible
5. File the completed log in the licensing binder (Section 5: Health)

> "Minor" does not mean undocumented. Every injury gets a log entry. A bump on the head that wasn't documented becomes a he-said-she-said problem if the parent notices it at home.

---

### Major Injuries (Medical Attention Required)

*Deep cuts requiring stitches, broken bones, head injuries with loss of consciousness, severe allergic reactions, any injury requiring emergency care.*

**Response sequence:**

1. **Call 911** if emergency — do not delay
2. Begin first aid or CPR if trained and appropriate
3. Send a staff member to meet paramedics at the entrance
4. **Call the parent immediately** — or emergency contact if parent is unreachable
5. A staff member accompanies the child to the hospital if the parent cannot arrive quickly
6. Complete a detailed injury/accident report
7. **Notify admin immediately**
8. **Notify licensing within 24 hours** if the injury required hospitalization
9. Follow up with the family — same day and next day

---

### When Licensing Must Be Notified

| Situation | Notification Required? | Timeframe |
|-----------|----------------------|------------|
| Child requires hospitalization | Yes | Within 24 hours |
| Child death on premises | Yes | Immediately |
| Allegation of child abuse or neglect by staff | Yes | Immediately |
| Major injury without hospitalization | Director judgment + admin | Same day to admin |
| Minor injury treated on-site | No licensing notice needed | Log in binder |

---

### What Every Incident Log Must Include

- **Date and time** of the incident
- **Child's name** and age
- **Description** — what happened, exactly, in plain language
- **Location** — which classroom, playground area, hallway
- **Staff present** at the time
- **Action taken** — how the injury was treated
- **Parent notification** — time called, who was spoken to, what was said
- **Staff member signature** who completed the form
- **Director signature**

---

### Child Abduction & Missing Child Protocol

If a child cannot be accounted for:

1. Immediately search the classroom, bathrooms, and adjacent areas
2. Notify the director — initiate a full building search
3. If not found within **5 minutes**, call 911
4. Lock all doors; account for all other children
5. Call the parent and admin
6. Review camera footage if available
7. Provide all information to police
8. **Do not speak to media** — refer all inquiries to admin

#### Unauthorized Pick-Up Attempt

- Verify identity and check the authorized pick-up list
- If the person is not on the list: politely refuse and call the custodial parent for authorization
- If the person becomes aggressive or forceful: **call 911 immediately**
- **Do not release the child under any circumstances** without explicit parent authorization — even if the person claims to be a grandparent, family friend, or has been before

---

### The Documentation Mindset

Every performance conversation, every parent concern, every incident, every near-miss — logged in ProCare or on the appropriate paper form, same day it happens.

> If it happened but isn't documented, it functionally didn't happen — legally and in any dispute. Your notes protect the child, the staff, and you.

scenarios.situation: During outdoor play, a 4-year-old trips and hits his head on the edge of the climbing structure. He cried briefly, calmed down within 2 minutes, and appears alert and normal. There's a small red mark but no cut or swelling. His teacher tells you about it at 10:30 AM. Pick-up isn't until 5:30 PM.
scenarios.question: What is the correct response?

options.label: Monitor him through the day and mention it to the parent at pick-up
options.feedback: Head injuries — even mild-appearing ones — require parent notification before pick-up, not at it. A parent who finds out at 5:30 PM about a 10:30 AM head bump will reasonably ask why they weren't called. You also lose 7 hours of potential observation time that the parent might have wanted to be aware of. Call now.
options.link: Call parents before pick-up — never save incident notifications for the end of the day.

options.label: Complete the injury log immediately, call the parent now, send a Tadpoles note, and continue monitoring him
options.feedback: Exactly right. Log it immediately while the details are fresh, call the parent to inform them ('He's fine and alert, but I wanted to make sure you knew right away'), send a Tadpoles note, and keep eyes on him through the day. If anything changes — vomiting, drowsiness, unusual behavior — you call again and escalate. This is textbook minor injury protocol.
options.link: Minor injury sequence: log → call parent → Tadpoles note → monitor.

options.label: Call 911 — any head injury in a child must be evaluated by paramedics
options.feedback: A 2-minute cry followed by full alertness and normal behavior is a minor injury presentation, not an emergency. Calling 911 for every bump would be clinically and operationally unworkable. Know the difference: loss of consciousness, vomiting after impact, unusual drowsiness, or severe swelling warrant emergency response. Alert, calm, no visible damage — monitor and call the parent.
options.link: Reserve 911 for true emergencies — know your minor vs. major injury thresholds.

options.label: Log the incident and notify the parent at pick-up to avoid alarming them unnecessarily
options.feedback: Parent notification happens before pick-up, not at it — and 'avoiding alarm' is not a reason to delay. Parents have the right to know their child was injured the same day it happens. Delaying the call until pick-up looks like concealment, erodes trust, and violates Bright Beginnings protocol. Call now.
options.link: Never delay parent notification to avoid a difficult conversation.

scenarios.situation: At 2:00 PM you receive a call from a staff member that a 2-year-old in the toddler room has had a severe allergic reaction — hives spreading rapidly, facial swelling starting, and she's having difficulty breathing. The child has a known peanut allergy on file.
scenarios.question: What is your immediate first action?

options.label: Call the parent first to ask if the child has an EpiPen
options.feedback: Do not call the parent first when a child is having difficulty breathing. Airway compromise is a life-threatening emergency — every second matters. Call 911 first. While paramedics are en route, check the child's medical action plan for EpiPen authorization (it should already be in the binder and accessible in the classroom). Parent call comes second.

options.label: Call 911 immediately, then check for an EpiPen in the child's medical action plan, then call the parent
options.feedback: Correct — this is the exact sequence for a life-threatening medical emergency. Call 911 immediately and describe the symptoms. While staff keeps the child calm and another staff member meets paramedics at the entrance, check the medical action plan for EpiPen instructions (trained staff administer if authorized). Call the parent simultaneously or immediately after 911. Notify admin the same day.
options.link: Medical emergency sequence: 911 → first aid per action plan → parent → admin.

options.label: Send the child to your office and wait to see if symptoms worsen
options.feedback: Facial swelling and difficulty breathing in an allergic reaction is anaphylaxis — a medical emergency that can become fatal within minutes without intervention. There is no 'wait and see' here. Call 911 immediately.
options.link: Anaphylaxis is a 911 emergency — act immediately.

options.label: Administer the EpiPen if one is on file, then call the parent
options.feedback: If the child has a medical action plan that authorizes EpiPen use by trained staff, that's an appropriate intervention — but calling 911 must happen at the same time or before, not after. EpiPen administration buys time; it doesn't replace emergency care. The correct sequence is 911 first, action plan second, parent third.
options.link: EpiPen + 911 together — not EpiPen instead of 911.

sections.title: Emergency Procedures
learningObjectives: Walk through the correct response to fire, tornado, and lockdown emergencies
Know what triggers facility closure for utility failures
Know what goes in every classroom emergency backpack
sections.content:
## Every Emergency Has a Sequence — Know Yours Before You Need It

In an emergency, you don't have time to think — you act on what's already in your head. These procedures exist so that every person in your building knows their role before anything happens.

---

### Fire Emergency

**When the alarm sounds:**

1. Evacuate immediately to the designated outdoor assembly area — no exceptions, no "let me check first"
2. Teachers grab **attendance clipboards** and **emergency backpacks** before leaving the room
3. Director or closer grabs **sign-in/out sheets** and their **phone**
4. Account for all children and staff at the assembly area
5. Call 911 if a real fire (not a drill)
6. **Do not re-enter the building** until the fire department gives all-clear
7. If the building is unsafe, relocate to your site's backup location

**Backup relocation sites:**
- Forest Lakes: Mill Creek location
- Mill Creek: Forest Lakes location
- Crozet: Local church or community center (confirm current address with admin)

**Parent notification during evacuation:**
- Send Tadpoles message with relocation information immediately
- Group Me to all staff
- Post on Facebook and website if relocation is extended

---

### Severe Weather (Tornado)

**Tornado watch** (conditions possible — not immediate):
- Monitor weather closely
- Prepare to move to interior hallways if a warning is issued
- Send parents a Tadpoles heads-up

**Tornado warning** (take shelter immediately):

1. Move all children and staff to interior hallways — away from windows and exterior walls
2. Everyone crouches down and covers their head with their hands
3. Take attendance
4. Stay in position until the warning expires or an all-clear is issued
5. If the building is damaged, evacuate to the backup location
6. Notify parents immediately via Tadpoles and phone calls

---

### Lockdown (Intruder or Threat on Campus)

**Immediate steps:**

1. Director announces lockdown over intercom or Group Me — all staff act immediately
2. All classroom doors locked
3. Lights off; children moved away from doors and windows
4. Silence — keep children calm
5. **Do not open the door for anyone** except police with badge verification shown through a window
6. Director calls 911 and provides location and information
7. Wait for police all-clear before unlocking

**After lockdown:**
- Account for all children and staff
- Notify parents via Tadpoles and phone
- Debrief with staff as soon as safely possible
- Offer support resources if needed
- Document and report to licensing if required

---

### Utility Failures: When to Close

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Power outage | Use emergency lighting; if over 2 hours or during extreme heat/cold — **close facility** |
| Water outage | **Close immediately** — cannot operate without toilets or handwashing |
| HVAC failure | Monitor temperature; if below 50°F or above 85°F — **close facility** |

> When in doubt about utility failures: call admin, then call parents. Never try to operate in an environment that cannot safely support children.

---

### General Evacuation Procedure

**Reasons to evacuate:**
Fire or smoke, gas leak, bomb threat, building structural damage (earthquake, tornado), chemical or hazardous material spill

**Sequence:**

1. Sound alarm or announce evacuation
2. Grab attendance clipboards, emergency backpacks, sign-in/out sheets
3. Exit via the nearest safe route to the outdoor assembly area
4. Take attendance — report any missing persons immediately
5. Call 911 if needed
6. Notify parents via Tadpoles with relocation information
7. Relocate to backup site if building is unsafe
8. Do not return until authorities give all-clear

---

### Emergency Backpack Contents (One Per Classroom)

Every classroom emergency backpack must contain:

- Attendance clipboard with current roster
- Emergency contact list for all children in that room
- First aid kit
- Bottled water and non-perishable snacks
- Flashlight and batteries
- Whistle
- Emergency procedures card

Check backpack contents **monthly** — supplies expire, rosters change, batteries die.

---

### Drill Schedule

| Drill Type | Frequency | Documentation |
|------------|-----------|---------------|
| Fire | Monthly | Log in licensing binder: date, time, duration, issues |
| Tornado | Monthly (alternate months with fire) | Log in licensing binder |
| Lockdown | Quarterly | Log in licensing binder; note any issues |

Drills must be documented the **same day they occur** — not end of the week, not when you get around to it.

questions.question: During a fire evacuation, what three things must the director or closer grab before leaving the building?
options: The licensing binder, first aid kit, and classroom rosters
Sign-in/out sheets, their phone, and the emergency backpack
Sign-in/out sheets and their phone
The emergency binder, car keys, and medication log
questions.correctAnswer: Sign-in/out sheets and their phone
questions.explanation: The director or closer grabs the sign-in/out sheets and their phone. Teachers grab attendance clipboards and emergency backpacks from their rooms. These roles are distinct — the director is not responsible for classroom backpacks. Sign-in/out sheets tell you exactly who was in the building; your phone lets you call 911 and parents.

questions.question: At what indoor temperature must the facility close due to HVAC failure?
options: Below 60°F or above 80°F
Below 55°F or above 90°F
Below 50°F or above 85°F
Below 45°F or above 95°F
questions.correctAnswer: Below 50°F or above 85°F
questions.explanation: Facility must close if indoor temperature drops below 50°F or rises above 85°F due to HVAC failure. These are not comfortable-versus-uncomfortable thresholds — they're safety thresholds. At these extremes, children are at risk of hypothermia or heat-related illness. Close, call admin, call parents.

questions.question: True or False: During a lockdown, a staff member may open a locked classroom door if they recognize the person knocking.
questions.correctAnswer: False
questions.explanation: False. During a lockdown, doors stay locked until police provide an all-clear — no exceptions. Recognition is not sufficient verification. Intruders can appear familiar, can claim to be a parent or familiar vendor, or can coerce someone to stand at the door. The protocol is clear: badge verification through a window from law enforcement only. Hold the line.

questions.question: How often must lockdown drills be conducted and documented?
options: Monthly
Every other month
Quarterly
Twice a year
questions.correctAnswer: Quarterly
questions.explanation: Lockdown drills are conducted quarterly — four times per year. Fire and tornado drills are monthly (alternating). Every drill must be documented in the licensing binder the same day it occurs: date, time, duration, and any issues noted. Missing drill documentation is one of the most common licensing findings.

sections.title: Classroom Setup Standards
learningObjectives: Know what must be posted in the lobby and in every classroom
Understand the bleach concentration requirements for each use
Know the nap policies, infant room regulations, and medicine box rules
sections.content:
## Physical Standards: What Every Room Must Have

Virginia licensing and Bright Beginnings standards require specific postings, setups, and supplies in every classroom. A walk-in inspection can happen any day. These standards must be met every day — not just before a known visit.

---

### Lobby Postings (Required)

The following must be visibly posted in the lobby at all times:

- Current Virginia childcare license (original)
- Most recent licensing inspection report
- Grievance policy and parent complaint procedure
- Staff/child ratios by age group
- Emergency evacuation map (with lobby exit marked)
- Fire drill and emergency drill log (current month)
- Daily menu (current week)
- Bright Beginnings center phone number and director contact

---

### Classroom Postings (Required in Every Room)

- Current lesson plan for the week
- Daily schedule (posted at child eye level and adult eye level)
- Emergency evacuation route from that specific room
- Child-to-staff ratio for that age group
- Allergy list (children in that room — no full names per privacy; use first name only or coded format per center policy)
- Attendance clipboard (current roster)
- Bleach solution labels (concentration, date mixed, use)

---

### Bleach Concentration Reference

Bleach solutions must be made fresh daily. Test with litmus paper and label each bottle.

| Use | Concentration | Ratio |
|-----|--------------|-------|
| Table/surface sanitizing (between uses) | 50–200 ppm | ~1 tbsp bleach per gallon water |
| Toy sanitizing (mouthed toys, daily) | 200 ppm | ~2 tbsp bleach per gallon water |
| Diaper changing surface | 200 ppm | ~2 tbsp bleach per gallon water |
| Blood/body fluid spills | 500–600 ppm | ~3 tbsp bleach per gallon water |
| Food contact surfaces (before meal prep) | 50–100 ppm | ~½ tbsp bleach per gallon water |
| Bathroom surfaces (toilets, sinks) | 200 ppm | ~2 tbsp bleach per gallon water |

**Note:** Label every bottle with: concentration, date mixed, and intended use. Never reuse a solution from a prior day.

---

### Classroom Setup Minimums

| Item | Minimum Standard |
|------|------------------|
| Books per child in room | 3 books available per child (not stored, actively accessible) |
| Baby dolls / play dolls | 5 dolls minimum; must represent diverse skin tones |
| Dramatic play items | Sufficient to support 3–4 children simultaneously |
| Manipulatives (blocks, puzzles, etc.) | Minimum 3 distinct types per age group |
| Art materials accessible | At least 1 medium available daily (crayons, paint, clay, etc.) |
| Sensory table | Active with appropriate materials; changed weekly minimum |

---

### Diapering: 6-Step Procedure

Every diaper change must follow this sequence — no shortcuts:

1. **Prepare the station** — gather supplies before bringing child; lay disposable paper on changing table
2. **Bring child to station** — keep one hand on child at all times; never leave them unattended
3. **Remove and bag soiled diaper** — fold it closed; place in sealed bin immediately
4. **Clean child thoroughly** — wipe front to back; dispose of all wipes in the sealed bin
5. **Apply fresh diaper** — dress child; return to classroom
6. **Clean and sanitize the surface** — disposable paper off; spray and wipe with 200 ppm bleach solution; wash hands

Post the 6-step procedure at the diaper station.

---

### Nap Policies

- **No nap mats on the floor before 12:45 PM** — mats out early cause chaos and signal to children that rest time is beginning before it is
- **Blinds must be UP during nap time** (not drawn) — this is a Virginia licensing requirement for visual supervision
- **Lights must remain ON** (or on the low, amber setting if your site has it) — dark rooms create supervision concerns for licensing
- **Staff must be actively monitoring** during nap — no phones, no paperwork at the expense of supervision
- Children who do not sleep must have a quiet alternative activity available (books, soft toys)

---

### Infant Room Regulations

- **Back to sleep:** All infants must be placed on their backs to sleep — no exceptions, no parent notes overriding this without a physician's written order
- **No propping bottles:** Staff may not prop a bottle for an infant — feeding must be supervised and held
- **Whiteboard documentation:** Each infant's feeding, sleep, and diaper change times are logged on the room's whiteboard in real time and transferred to Tadpoles notes daily
- **Grab and Go Bag:** The infant room must maintain a fully stocked Grab and Go Bag near the door at all times — contains: infant roster, emergency contacts, formula/food as labeled, diapers, wipes, first aid basics, and a blanket per child

---

### First Aid Kit Requirements

**Inventory requirement by location:**

| Location | Number of First Aid Kits Required |
|----------|----------------------------------|
| Forest Lakes | 3 (1 per floor + 1 in office) |
| Mill Creek | 2 (1 classroom wing + 1 office) |
| Crozet | 2 (1 classroom wing + 1 office) |

Every kit must contain (at minimum): adhesive bandages (multiple sizes), sterile gauze pads, medical tape, antiseptic wipes, disposable gloves (multiple pairs), cold pack, emergency contact list for that area.

Check kits **monthly** — restock any depleted items immediately.

---

### Medicine Box Policy

> **ONLY two types of medication may be stored at the center:**

1. **Prescription inhaler** (with current medical action plan on file)
2. **EpiPen** (with current medical action plan and allergy documentation on file)

**No over-the-counter medications** may be stored at the center or administered by staff — not Tylenol, not Benadryl, not cough syrup. If a parent requests OTC medication administration, direct them to the AMAT/MAT policy and contact admin.

**AMAT staff** may administer only the above two medications, only with current signed authorization on file, and only per the documented medical action plan.

---

### 72-Hour Emergency Kit Contents

Every site must maintain a 72-hour emergency supply kit, stored near the main exit:

- 1 gallon water per person (staff + child count at peak enrollment)
- Non-perishable food (granola bars, dried fruit, crackers) — sufficient for 72 hours
- Manual can opener
- Flashlights (2+) with extra batteries
- Battery-powered or hand-crank radio
- Emergency blankets (1 per person)
- First aid kit (separate from classroom kits)
- Waterproof copy of all emergency contact lists
- Infant supplies if applicable (formula, diapers, wipes)
- Basic tools: duct tape, rope, work gloves
- Whistle (1 per adult)

Check and replenish contents **annually** at minimum. Mark expiration dates on all food items.

questions.question: During nap time, what is the rule about window blinds?
options: Blinds must be fully closed to help children sleep
Blinds must remain UP (open) for visual supervision compliance
Blinds are optional based on room setup
Blinds should be half-open for light control
questions.correctAnswer: Blinds must remain UP (open) for visual supervision compliance
questions.explanation: Blinds must be UP during nap time — this is a Virginia licensing requirement. Closed blinds prevent visual supervision from outside the room and can be cited as a violation. Lights must also remain on (or at the low amber setting). Dark, closed rooms during nap are a common licensing finding.

questions.question: What bleach concentration is used to sanitize a diaper changing surface?
options: 50 ppm
100 ppm
200 ppm
600 ppm
questions.correctAnswer: 200 ppm
questions.explanation: Diaper changing surfaces require 200 ppm bleach solution — approximately 2 tablespoons of bleach per gallon of water. This is the same concentration used for mouthed toys and bathroom surfaces. Higher concentrations (500–600 ppm) are reserved for blood/body fluid spills. Solutions must be made fresh daily and labeled with concentration, date, and intended use.

questions.question: A parent brings in liquid Tylenol and a signed note asking staff to give their child a dose at 2 PM. What is the correct response?
options: Accept it if the note is signed by the parent
Accept it only if the child has a current medical action plan on file
Decline — only prescription inhalers and EpiPens are permitted at the center
Accept it if an AMAT-certified staff member is on shift
questions.correctAnswer: Decline — only prescription inhalers and EpiPens are permitted at the center
questions.explanation: Only two types of medication may be stored or administered at the center: prescription inhalers and EpiPens — both requiring a current medical action plan. Over-the-counter medications including Tylenol, Benadryl, and cough syrup are NOT permitted, regardless of parent authorization or AMAT certification. Direct the parent to the policy and contact admin if they push back.

questions.question: How many books must be actively accessible to children in a classroom?
options: 5 books total per classroom
3 books per child in the room
1 book per child, stored on a shelf
10 books minimum regardless of class size
questions.correctAnswer: 3 books per child in the room
questions.explanation: The standard is 3 books per child — not 3 books total. A classroom of 10 children must have at least 30 books actively accessible (not stored in a cabinet). These must be age-appropriate, in good condition, and accessible without adult assistance during free choice time.

questions.question: True or False: In the infant room, a staff member may prop a bottle for an infant if no other staff are available at that moment.
questions.correctAnswer: False
questions.explanation: False — no exceptions, no circumstances. Propped bottles are both a safety hazard (choking, aspiration) and a regulatory violation. Bottle feeding must always be supervised and held. If staffing is insufficient to individually feed an infant, that is a ratio violation that must be addressed immediately — not worked around.

sections.title: Situational Protocols
learningObjectives: Know the exact response sequence for the most common non-emergency situations
Understand the snow day decision process and who makes the call
Know how to handle a licensing inspector walk-in and an irate parent
sections.content:
## Situations That Happen More Than You Think

These aren't full emergencies — but they require a practiced, confident response. Directors who haven't thought through these moments make them worse. Directors who have a protocol execute cleanly.

---

### Snow Day / Weather Closure

**Decision authority:** The owner makes the final closure decision. Directors do not close independently — you call admin first.

**Snow day decision sequence:**

1. Monitor weather from the prior evening
2. If conditions are questionable by 5:00 AM, text admin immediately — do not wait for 6:00 AM
3. Admin evaluates road conditions, school closures (ACS), and center safety
4. Decision is communicated to directors no later than 6:00 AM if possible
5. Director sends the closure message via Tadpoles and posts to Facebook within 15 minutes of decision

**Standard Closure Message Template:**

> *"Good morning, Bright Beginnings families! Due to [weather/road conditions], [Location Name] will be CLOSED today, [Day, Date]. We look forward to seeing your family [tomorrow / on our next scheduled day]. Please stay safe and warm! — [Director Name]"*

**If closing early due to deteriorating conditions:** Same notification process, but also call parents of any children still in care personally if the situation is developing quickly.

---

### Licensing Inspector Walk-In

Licensing inspectors may visit unannounced at any time during operating hours. The correct response is professional and calm — not panicked.

1. **Ask for their business card** — get their name, title, and agency before anything else
2. **Call admin immediately** — text or call while the inspector is signing in: *"Licensing inspector here for a walk-in. [Inspector name], VDSS."* Admin wants to know in real time.
3. **Welcome them professionally** — *"We're happy to have you. I'll be with you momentarily."* Finish the urgent call with admin (30 seconds max), then give them your full attention.
4. **Walk with them** — do not let an inspector walk rooms alone. Stay present and available throughout.
5. **Answer questions directly** — if you don't know an answer, say *"Let me get that for you"* and find it. Never guess.
6. **Pull the licensing binder immediately** — offer it proactively: *"Here's our licensing binder — everything is current as of [last check date]."*
7. **After the visit:** Document the visit, the inspector's name, areas reviewed, and any preliminary comments in your Director Packet and notify admin with a full report the same day.

---

### Unauthorized or Suspicious Vehicle

If a vehicle parks in your lot and the occupant does not enter the building or appears to be watching children:

1. **Do not approach the vehicle**
2. Note the make, model, color, and license plate number
3. Call **911** if the vehicle remains for more than a few minutes or the behavior is clearly suspicious
4. Notify admin
5. Do not take children outside until the situation is resolved
6. Document the incident in the Director Packet

---

### Towing Unauthorized Vehicles

For vehicles parked in the lot without authorization (not a safety emergency):

| Location | Towing Company | Phone |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| Albemarle County area | Albemarle Towing | 434-974-7546 |
| Downtown/Crozet area | Collier's Towing | 434-293-6433 |

Post the approved towing company contact on the front desk reference card. Do not call any random towing service.

---

### Irate Parent — In Person

When a parent arrives upset and escalating:

**Four-step response:**

1. **Remove from the lobby** — *"Let's step into my office where we can talk privately."* An upset parent in the lobby affects other families and staff. Get them out of the public space immediately.
2. **Listen without defending** — let them speak without interruption until they've said everything. Do not argue facts in this moment.
3. **Acknowledge** — *"I hear you. I understand why you're frustrated. I want to make this right."* Acknowledgment is not admission — it's de-escalation.
4. **Commit to a resolution timeline** — *"I'm going to look into this today. I will call you by [specific time — within 1 hour]."* Then do it.

> **The 1-hour resolution window:** You have 1 hour to investigate and respond. Not end of day. Not tomorrow. Within the hour you committed to. If you need more time, call them at the committed time to update them and give a new time.

---

### Irate Parent — By Phone

Same four steps apply, adapted for phone:

1. **Let them speak** — don't interrupt or defend
2. **Acknowledge** — *"I completely understand. Thank you for calling me directly."*
3. **Offer to continue** — *"I want to give this my full attention. Are you able to hold for two minutes while I pull the notes, or would you prefer I call you right back?"*
4. **Commit to follow-through** — give a specific time for resolution and honor it

---

### Parent Termination (Ending a Family's Enrollment)

Occasionally, the director must end an enrollment — due to non-payment, safety violations, or a pattern of abusive behavior toward staff.

**Process:**

1. Consult with admin before initiating — do not terminate a family enrollment without admin awareness
2. Prepare written notice (template in Google Drive: Admin/Parent Termination Letter)
3. Hold an in-person meeting if possible; if not, deliver by certified mail and email simultaneously
4. State the reason clearly and briefly; do not debate
5. Give the family a transition timeline per the contract (typically 2 weeks unless safety requires immediate exit)
6. Process any outstanding balance before the final day
7. Deactivate Brivo access on the last day of enrollment
8. Document everything in ProCare

scenarios.situation: It's 5:45 AM. You've been monitoring a winter storm overnight. There are 3 inches of snow on the ground, roads appear icy, and Albemarle County Schools have already called a 2-hour delay. Admin has not texted you yet.
scenarios.question: What is the right action?

options.label: Post a closure message immediately — it's clearly unsafe to open
options.feedback: Directors do not close independently. Even when the situation seems obvious, the closure decision belongs to admin/ownership. Posting a closure without authorization could contradict admin's plan (they may have decided to open with a delay, for example). Text admin immediately instead.
options.link: Snow day decisions require admin authorization — always.

options.label: Text admin immediately with the current conditions, ACS delay status, and your read on the situation
options.feedback: Correct. Text admin now — don't wait for them to contact you. Give them: road conditions, ACS decision, your assessment. This lets admin make the call with full information. You're not making the decision; you're making sure they have what they need to make it quickly. Time matters — families need to know by 6:00 AM.
options.link: Directors initiate the conversation — admin makes the call.

options.label: Wait until 6:30 AM to see if conditions improve before deciding
options.feedback: Waiting until 6:30 AM means families are already in their cars, kids are dressed, and people are making decisions without information. By 6:00 AM, families need to know. If you haven't contacted admin by 5:45 AM and conditions are this clear, you're already behind.
options.link: The 6:00 AM communication deadline exists for families — work backward from it.

options.label: Open normally and send a safety-reminder message to parents
options.feedback: Opening in icy conditions without admin consultation is a liability decision that isn't yours to make. If a staff member or family member is in an accident on the way to your center, the center's decision to remain open is part of that story. Always loop in admin.
options.link: Center closure decisions have legal and safety implications — admin must be involved.

scenarios.situation: A licensing inspector walks in at 10:15 AM. Your licensing binder is on your desk, updated as of last week. Two classrooms are in ratio. One room has a substitute who arrived this morning — you haven't confirmed her paperwork is fully on file yet.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.label: Greet the inspector, ask for their card, text admin, then walk with the inspector — address the substitute situation as it comes up
options.feedback: Exactly right. You follow the protocol: get the card, text admin immediately, welcome them professionally, offer the binder proactively, and stay present throughout the walkthrough. If the inspector asks about the substitute's paperwork, answer honestly: 'We had a last-minute call-out this morning — I'm verifying her file is complete and will have that for you shortly.' Honesty and professionalism go further than scrambling to hide it.
options.link: Licensing protocol: card → admin text → walk with them → binder proactively.

options.label: Tell the inspector you need to call admin before you can allow the visit
options.feedback: You cannot delay or refuse a licensing walk-in — that would be a violation in itself. You can and should text admin while signing the inspector in (30 seconds), but you may not use 'I need to call admin' as a way to buy time or create a barrier to the inspection.
options.link: Walk-in inspections cannot be delayed — proceed professionally while texting admin.

options.label: Quickly pull the substitute from the classroom before the inspector sees the room
options.feedback: This would create a ratio violation. Never adjust your staffing in response to an inspection in a way that creates a new violation. The substitute situation is a documentation gap, not a safety violation (assuming she is cleared to work). Address it honestly, not by making the situation worse.
options.link: Never create a ratio violation to cover a documentation gap.

options.label: Focus entirely on the inspector and text admin after the visit
options.feedback: Admin needs to know in real time — not after. A one-sentence text while the inspector is signing in takes 10 seconds: 'Licensing walk-in. [Name], VDSS.' That's all you need to send. Admin may have information or context that affects your response. Notify first, then give the inspector your full attention.
options.link: Text admin in the first 60 seconds — then give full attention to the inspector.

sections.title: Field Trip Guide
learningObjectives: Know the seasonal field trip destinations and planning timeline
Understand the school bus rules and parent driver requirements
Know the Virginia regulation requirements for off-site activities (8VAC20-780-580)
sections.content:
## Field Trips: Planning, Safety, and Compliance

Field trips are some of the most memorable experiences we give children — and some of the highest-liability moments in childcare operations. The planning process is non-negotiable.

---

### Seasonal Field Trip Destinations

| Season | Destination Ideas |
|--------|------------------|
| Fall | Carter Mountain Apple Orchard, Chiles Peach Orchard, Monticello (older groups), pumpkin patch |
| Winter | Indoor play spaces, local libraries (story time events), local firehouses |
| Spring | Shenandoah Valley farms, botanical gardens, nature preserves, local parks |
| Summer | Splash pads, county parks, local zoos (Richmond Metro Zoo), community gardens |

All destinations must be pre-approved by admin before booking. New destinations require a director site visit before scheduling children.

---

### Planning Timeline

**1 Month Before the Trip:**
- [ ] Admin approval received for destination and date
- [ ] Parent permission slips designed and sent home (never verbal permission only)
- [ ] Bus or transportation confirmed and booked
- [ ] Ratio reviewed — confirm adequate staff for off-site requirements
- [ ] Emergency contact packets pulled for all attending children
- [ ] First aid kit and medication (inhalers/EpiPens) staged

**2 Weeks Before:**
- [ ] All permission slips returned; follow up on missing slips
- [ ] Headcount confirmed with the destination
- [ ] T-shirt order confirmed (see below)
- [ ] Parent driver screening completed if parents are driving (see below)
- [ ] Budget approved by admin

**1 Week Before:**
- [ ] Staff assignments finalized; no schedule changes without director approval
- [ ] Children's allergy and medical needs reviewed with all attending staff
- [ ] Group roster printed and laminated (one per adult)
- [ ] Parent Tadpoles message sent with logistics: date, destination, departure time, what to pack

**Day Of:**
- [ ] Attendance taken before departure and confirmed against permission slip list
- [ ] No child departs without a signed permission slip on file
- [ ] All medication (inhalers/EpiPens) in the field first aid kit
- [ ] All staff have emergency contact lists
- [ ] Director or designee does a final headcount before the bus or cars pull out

---

### T-Shirt Colors

All field trip groups wear Bright Beginnings t-shirts. Colors are assigned by role:

- **Children:** Light blue BB t-shirt
- **Teachers/Staff:** Hot pink BB t-shirt

This makes it immediately obvious who is a child vs. a supervising adult in any public space. Order shirts 2 weeks in advance; confirm sizes.

---

### School Bus Rules

- Albemarle County school buses are available **only for groups of 4 or more children**
- Groups under 4 may not use a county school bus — arrange alternate transportation
- Bus booking contact: admin handles — do not book buses independently
- All children must be seated and buckled (if applicable) before the bus departs
- Staff riding the bus: minimum 1 adult per 10 children plus 1 adult in the back row

---

### Parent Driver Rules

If parents volunteer to drive children on a field trip:

- Parent must provide **current proof of insurance** and a **valid driver's license** — copies on file before the trip
- Parent may drive **their own child only** unless explicitly approved by admin and other families consent in writing
- Parent drivers are not counted in the staff-to-child ratio
- Parent volunteers are required to complete the **Volunteer Driver Form** (Google Drive: Field Trips/Forms)

---

### Virginia Regulation: 8VAC20-780-580

Key requirements for off-site activities under Virginia licensing:

| Requirement | Standard |
|-------------|----------|
| Written parent permission | Required for every field trip; verbal permission is insufficient |
| Staff-to-child ratio | Must meet or exceed the normal classroom ratio at all times off-site |
| Emergency contacts | Must accompany the group — not remain at the center |
| First aid kit | Required at all times during the trip |
| Medication | Required medications (inhalers, EpiPens) must travel with the group |
| Supervision plan | Director must have a written supervision plan for each trip, including accountability at every transition |
| Return time | Families must be notified of any significant delay from planned return time |

questions.question: A parent calls to give verbal permission for their child to attend a field trip because they forgot to send back the paper slip. Can the child attend?
options: Yes — verbal permission from a parent is sufficient
Yes — if you document the call with date, time, and parent's name
No — Virginia regulation 8VAC20-780-580 requires written permission for every field trip
Yes — but only if admin approves the exception
questions.correctAnswer: No — Virginia regulation 8VAC20-780-580 requires written permission for every field trip
questions.explanation: Virginia licensing regulation 8VAC20-780-580 is explicit: written parent permission is required for every field trip. Verbal permission — even documented — does not meet the standard. If a slip isn't returned, the child does not go. Follow up with families early enough that there's time to get the slip back.

questions.question: What color t-shirts do children wear on field trips? What color do teachers wear?
options: Children: yellow; Teachers: blue
Children: light blue; Teachers: hot pink
Both wear the same color — white BB shirts
Children: hot pink; Teachers: light blue
questions.correctAnswer: Children: light blue; Teachers: hot pink
questions.explanation: Children wear light blue BB shirts; teachers and staff wear hot pink. The color distinction makes it immediately clear in any public space who is a child vs. a supervising adult — a safety and identification standard that makes group management much easier in crowded environments.

questions.question: A group of 3 children is scheduled for a community library visit. Can they use an Albemarle County school bus?
options: Yes — school buses are available for any group size
No — Albemarle County school buses are only available for groups of 4 or more
Yes — if a parent signs a waiver
No — school buses are only for K-12 students
questions.correctAnswer: No — Albemarle County school buses are only available for groups of 4 or more
questions.explanation: Albemarle County school buses are available only for groups of 4 or more children. For smaller groups, arrange alternative transportation. Bus booking goes through admin — directors do not book buses independently.

questions.question: A parent volunteers to drive her daughter and two classmates to a pumpkin patch. What must be on file before she can drive?
options: Nothing additional — parent volunteers don't need paperwork
A signed permission slip from each child's parent
Current proof of insurance, valid driver's license, and a completed Volunteer Driver Form
Admin approval and a background check
questions.correctAnswer: Current proof of insurance, valid driver's license, and a completed Volunteer Driver Form
questions.explanation: Parent volunteer drivers must provide: current proof of insurance, valid driver's license, and a completed Volunteer Driver Form (all on file before the trip). Additionally, a parent may only drive their own child and other children with explicit admin approval and written family consent.
